d: Adds new lines to a hologram. `insert` means adding after the specified line and moving the next lines below.
p:
append [((click|touch|interact)[-]able|interactive)] [line[s]] %strings/itemtypes% to [holo[gram][s]] %holograms%
prepend [((click|touch|interact)[-]able|interactive)] [line[s]] %strings/itemtypes% to [holo[gram][s]] %holograms%
insert [((click|touch|interact)[-]able|interactive)] [line[s]] %strings/itemtypes% in[to] [holo[gram][s]] %holograms% at line %number%
insert [((click|touch|interact)[-]able|interactive)] [line[s]] %strings/itemtypes% in[to] [holo[gram][s]] %holograms% at [the] %number%(st|nd|rd|th) line

d:
Deletes a hologram. The hologram type has the delete changer already, but this effect exists for variables. For example `delete {_holo}` would delete the variable but you can use `delete hologram {_holo}` to avoid that.

When you delete a hologram that is stored in a variable, the hologram object will still exist in the variable but will not be usable. You should delete the variable too in this case.
Makes a hologram start/stop following an entity.

t: Requests - The value of the request header

d: Teleports a hologram to a specific location.
Returns all the holograms created by skript-holo or other plugins *except HolographicDisplays*.
Returns the followed entity of a hologram. Use the `Following Hologram` effect to make an exiting hologram start/stop following an entity.
Returns offset (directions) of a following hologram. Can be set.
Returns the creation date of a hologram.
Checks if the given string is alphanumeric.
Allows you to get or change the map of a map state to be played on different maps
p:
[map] id of map state[s] %mapstates%
map state %mapstates%'[s] [map] id

d:
A line of a HolographicDisplays hologram. Can be deleted using the 'delete/clear' changer.

Has converters to `text`, `item type` and `number`. Converters mean that you can use this type like the converted types. For example you can do `give line 1 of hologram to player` and `if event-hologram-line is 5:`, but please note that getting line number of a hologram line will check every line of the hologram. You shouldn't make systems that relies on line numbers, but contents.
Returns the parent hologram of a hologram line.

This effect will make entity glow with specific color. To unglow use NONE as color.
All color names: BLACK(0, "0"), DARK_BLUE(1, "1"), DARK_GREEN(2, "2"), DARK_AQUA(3, "3"), DARK_RED(4, "4"), DARK_PURPLE(5, "5"), GOLD(6, "6"), GRAY(7, "7"), DARK_GRAY(8, "8"), BLUE(9, "9"), GREEN(10, "a"), AQUA(11, "b"), RED(12, "c"), PURPLE(13, "d"), YELLOW(14, "e"), WHITE(15, "f"), NONE(-1, "");
Checks whether the given players can see the given holograms. Use the `Hologram Visibility` effect to hide/show holograms.
p:
[holo[gram][s]] %holograms% (is|are) (visible|(invisible|hidden)) (for|to|from) %players%
%players% can see [holo[gram][s]] %holograms%
[holo[gram][s]] %holograms% (is|are)(n't| not) (visible|(invisible|hidden)) (for|to|from) %players%
%players% can('t| not) see [holo[gram][s]] %holograms%

d:
Creates a new hologram.

Besides the `store in %variable%`, the `Last Created Hologram` expression can be used to get the created hologram.
p:
(create|spawn) [a] [new] holo[gram] [with [((click|touch|interact)[-]able|interactive)] line[s] %strings/itemtypes%] [%direction% %location%] [and store [it] in [[the] variable] %objects%]
(create|spawn) [a] [new] holo[gram] [with [((click|touch|interact)[-]able|interactive)] line[s] %strings/itemtypes%] that follows %entity% [[with] offset [(of|by|in|to)] [[the] direction] %directions%] [and store [it] in [[the] variable] %objects%]

d:
Makes a hologram line click-able or/and touchable. 

- **Click-able:** when a player clicks on the line, the `Hologram Line Click` event will be triggered
- **Touchable:** when a player tries to pickup the item in the line, the `Hologram Line Touch` event will be triggered
- **Interactive:** includes both click-able and touchable

Returns content (text or item) of a hologram line. Please note that there are hologram line to text and item converters so you can use a hologram line like a text or an item (e.g. give first line of holo to player)

Returns the number of a hologram line. But please note that getting line number of a hologram line will check every line of the hologram. You shouldn't make systems that relies on line numbers, but contents.
p:
[the] line number[s] of [holo[gram] line[s]] %hologramlines%
%hologramlines%'[s] line number[s]

d:
Returns lines of a hologram. Can be changed, removing a text or an item means it will search lines that match exactly with the input and remove them.

It is possible to create new lines using the `set` changer, for example if the hologram has 3 lines and you set line 5, the line 4 will be blank.
p:
[all] [the] lines of [holo[gram][s]] %holograms%
%holograms%'[s] [all] lines
[the] line %number% of [holo[gram][s]] %holograms%
[the] %number%(st|nd|rd|th) line[s] of [holo[gram][s]] %holograms%
[the] (first|last) line[s] of [holo[gram][s]] %holograms%

d:
Holds the hologram that was created most recently with the `Create Hologram` effect.
Can be deleted using the `delete/clear` changer which means the hologram will be removed from the world and this expression will be empty.

A HolographicDisplays hologram. Can be deleted using the `delete/clear` changer or can be reset using the `reset` changer. When you delete a hologram that is stored in a variable, the hologram object will still exist in the variable but will not be usable. You should delete the variable too in this case.
Returns the author/from part of an email. Can be set in a email scope

Called when a Citizens NPC is left clicked by a player.
Only is called if NPC is not vulnerable.

This effect will spawn specified particle with given options. It will be spawned for everyone
If chunk will be not loaded, it will not spawn particle, so it is safe and it will not affect your TPS that much like normal spawning may do.
Use bukkit "Particle" enum names as particle name. You can use dots and name do not have to be upper cased.
List of names is here: https://hub.spigotmc.org/javadocs/spigot/org/bukkit/Particle.html
p:
[morkazsk] (summon|play|create|activate|spawn) %number% [of] [particle] %string%:%number% offset (at|by|from) %number%, %number%(,| and) %number% at %location%
[morkazsk] (summon|play|create|activate|spawn) %number% [of] [particle] %string% offset (at|by|from) %number%, %number%(,| and) %number% with extra [data] %number% at %location%

d:
It creates a new gui with a given id (optional), using a base inventory, and a shape (optional)
For more info, read [this](https://github.com/Tuke-Nuke/TuSKe/wiki/GUI-Manager)
Checks if goat has the left, right, both or either horn.
p:
%livingentities% (has|have) [its|the[ir]] (left|right|either|any) [goat] horn
%livingentities% (doesn't|does not|do not|don't) have [its|the[ir]] (left|right|either|any) [goat] horn
%livingentities% (has|have) [its|the[ir]|both] [goat] horns
%livingentities% (doesn't|does not|do not|don't) have [its|the[ir]|both] [goat] horns

d:
Change visibility of a player for the given players.
When reveal is used in combination of the hidden players expression and the viewers are not specified, this will default it to the given player in the hidden players expression.

Note: if a player was hidden and relogs, this player will be visible again.

The time a furnace takes to burn an item in a fuel burn event.
Can also be used to change the burn/cook time of a placed furnace.

Returns whether the item frame is "fixed" or not.
When true it's not possible to destroy/move the frame (e. g. by damage, interaction, pistons, or missing supporting blocks), rotate the item or place/remove items.
p:
item[ |-]frame %entities% (is|are) fixed
item[ |-]frame %entities% (isn't|is not|aren't|are not) fixed

d: Called when a Citizens NPC is removed.

Apply or remove potion effects to/from entities.

**REMOVED in Skript 2.14.0+**
p:
apply %potioneffects% to %livingentities%
apply infinite [ambient] [potion of] %potioneffecttypes% [potion] [[[of] tier] %number%] [without [any] particles] [(whilst hiding [the]|without (the|a)) [potion] icon] to %livingentities% [replacing [the] existing effect]
apply [ambient] [potion of] %potioneffecttypes% [potion] [[[of] tier] %number%] [without [any] particles] [(whilst hiding [the]|without (the|a)) [potion] icon] to %livingentities% [for %timespan%] [replacing [the] existing effect]
c: 2.0, 2.2-dev27 (ambient and particle-less potion effects), 2.5 (replacing existing effect), 2.5.2 (potion effects), 2.7 (icon and infinite)

d: Returns the author of target points.

t: Item Frame - is Visible / is Invisible
d: Returns whether the item frame is visible or not.

p: [skDragon] drawSpircle %number% %particlename% particle[s] [rotating] at %entity/locations% with id %string%, %number% point[s], density %number%, radius %number% and speed %number%[, offset %number%, %number%, %number%][, rotate %number%, %number%, %number%][, visibleTo %players%][, visibleRange %number%][, loop delay %number%]

d: Using the names in the CustomEmotes.yml file you can play any custom emote or animation sequence you've made
Using the names in the CustomEmotes.yml file you can play any custom emote or animation sequence you've made

Stops an emote effect
Removes the red tint from a player
Sends red tint to a player. (Needs graphics set to fancy or in optifine vignettes)
p: [skDragon] send[ red] tint to %players%[ with fadeTime %number%, intensity %number% and damageMode %boolean%]

Returns the team of target points.

If it isn't inside an email scope, this expression returns a new Email. If it is inside of an email scope, it returns the email that belongs to that scope.
Returns a internet address from a string.

d: An assortment of different effects with a few shapes that play certain effects, some of which may or may not actually be considered 'warp' effects. I went a little bit nuts when I created this one, there are over 35 styles in it. Added in 0.09.0-BETA
p: [skDragon] drawWarpRings style %number%, particle %string%[, material %itemstack%][, speed %number%][, ([offset]XYZ|RGB) %number%, %number%, %number%], center %entity/location%, id %string%[, isSingle %boolean%, %player%][, r[ainbow]M[ode] %boolean%], scan %boolean%, height %number%, radius %number%, ringCount %number%, ringDensity %number%, visibleRange %number%[, rot[ation]XYZ %number%, %number%, %number%][, dis[placement]XYZ %number%, %number%, %number%][, pulseDelay %number%][, keepFor %timespan%[, repeat %number%]]

d:
Draws a wing trail that follows the player or plays them at a location. New Syntax as of v0.06.0-BETA Added flapping animation in v0.10.0-BETA

4 colored version: https://skripthub.net/docs/?id=3633
p: [skDragon] drawWings[ style] %number%, particle1 %string%[[, material] %itemstack%][, speed %number%][, ([offset]XYZ|RGB) %number%, %number%, %number%], particle2 %string%[[, material] %itemstack%][, speed2 %number%][, ([offset]XYZ2|RGB2) %number%, %number%, %number%], particle3 %string%[[, material] %itemstack%][, speed3 %number%][, ([offset]XYZ3|RGB3) %number%, %number%, %number%], center %entity/location%, id %string%[, isSingle %boolean%, %player%][, r[ainbow]M[ode] %boolean%][, flapSpeed %number%, flapRange %number%], angle %number%, height %number%, space %number%, visibleRange %number%[, tps %number%, second %number%]

d: Lists all currently running effects
Gets a list of all active id names used
Gets a list of all system fonts for use in drawText
Draws a cylinder that follows the player or plays at a location. New as of v0.07.0-Beta
p: [skDragon] drawCylinder particle %string%[, material %itemstack%][, speed %number%][, ([offset]XYZ|RGB) %number%, %number%, %number%], center %entity/location%, id %string%[, isSingle %boolean%, %player%][, r[ainbow]M[ode] %boolean%], randomRotation %boolean%, isSolid %boolean%, radius %number%, density %number%, height %number%, sideRatio %number%, visibleRange %number%[, xR[otation] %number%, yR[otation] %number%, zR[otation] %number%][, dis[placement]X %number%, dis[placement]Y %number%, dis[placement]Z %number%][, tps %number%, second %number%]

d: Draws a randomized two colored planet that follows the player or plays at a location. New as of v0.07.0-Beta
p: [skDragon] drawPlanet particle1 %string%[[, material] %itemstack%][, speed %number%][, ([offset]XYZ|RGB) %number%, %number%, %number%], particle2 %string%[[, material] %itemstack%][, speed2 %number%][, ([offset]XYZ2|RGB2) %number%, %number%, %number%], center %entity/location%, id %string%[, isSingle %boolean%, %player%][, r[ainbow]M[ode] %boolean%][, rotation %boolean%,[ plane] %string%, rot[ation]Speed %number%][, orbit %boolean%, orbit[al]Radius %number%, orbit[al]Step %number%[, xR[otation] %number%, yR[otation] %number%, zR[otation] %number%]], radius %number%, density %number%, precision %number%, bumpHeight %number%, visibleRange %number%[, dis[placement]X %number%, dis[placement]Y %number%, dis[placement]Z %number%][, tps %number%, second %number%]

d:
Create a new CMO (custom merchant object), that can be manipulated, edited and opened to a player.
Requires a name.

Gets whether the provided projectiles have left the hitbox of their shooter and can now hit entities.
This is recalculated each tick if the projectile has a shooter.
p:
%projectiles% (has|have) [already] left [the[ir]|its] shooter
%projectiles% (doesn't|does not|do not|don't) have [already] left [the[ir]|its] shooter

d:
Scoreboard tags are simple list of texts stored directly in the data of an entity.
So this is a Minecraft related thing, not Bukkit, so the tags will not get removed when the server stops. You can visit visit Minecraft Wiki for more info.
This is changeable and valid for any type of entity. Also you can use use the Has Scoreboard Tag condition to check whether an entity has the given tags.

Requires Minecraft 1.11+ (actually added in 1.9 to the game, but added in 1.11 to Spigot).
p:
[(all [[of] the]|the)] scoreboard tags of %entities%
%entities%'[s] scoreboard tags

d: Get the current recipe count of a merchant object or a villager/wandering trader.
p:
[the] (recipe|trade) count of merchant %merchant%
[the] (recipe|trade) count of villager %entity%

d:
Open a merchant or villager/wandering trader to players.
This allows them to trade, following the normal villager rules.

Traditionally, villagers can only trade with a single player at a time. This syntax allows you to force multiple players.
p:
open merchant %merchant% to %players%
open villager %entity% to %players%

d: Checks if the provided signs can be edited.

Creates a new MerchantRecipe object, that can be added to a merchant, villager or wandering trader.
The syntax requires three singular items. Air can be used in place of the second item.

Trades using Air as the first or third ingredient cannot be completed.

MerchantRecipes created have 99999 uses and provide no experience by default. This is due to issues created with experience from a custom merchant object.

Open one of the new 1.14 containers to a player as a GUI.
Also includes some old containers that were missing from vanilla Skript, such as fake ender chests, hoppers, dispensers and droppers.

Some of these can be used for their normal crafting. However, some of them might cause issues. I have done my best to restore their function.

You can set the name of each, which is displayed at the top. Fake lecterns do not display the name and currently have no useful function.
p:
open (barrel|lectern|loom|cartography (table|gui)|stone[ ]cutter|dispenser|dropper|hopper) named %string% to %players%
open [fake] (smoker|blast furnace|ender chest|grindstone) named %string% to %players%

d:
Lock a container, using a text key.
The name of an item functions as the key to open it.

Bukkit broke this effect in 1.14, and the workaround used here is a little temperamental.

Checks whether a container (tile entity such as barrel, chest, etc.) has a lock.
Use the lock effect to lock it.

Draws a client-side guardian laser, for a number of seconds.
This is static and does not damage entities. It is purely visual.
p: draw (guardian beam|laser) from %location% to %location% [[and] keep] for %integer% second[s]

d:
This shoots a snowball with a custom item model on it, of the item used in the syntax.
1.14 allows the snowball texture to be changed using the "Item" NBT tag.

Make an insentient entity walk to a location.
This works for most mobs.
It does NOT work for players or armour stands, or things that don't walk.

Maximum distance is defined by the entity tracking range in the config.

Entities may lose interest and wander off.
p:
make %livingentities% (pathfind|walk) to %location% [at speed %-number%]
make %livingentities% (pathfind|walk) to %entity% [at speed %-number%]

d:
Called frequently when a player is riding any entity.
event-string returns the key pressed.
The keys are "W", "A", "S", "D", "SHIFT" and "SPACEBAR".
If no key is pressed, the event-string will be "NONE".
This currently is not cancellable, due to how my listener is implemented.
If you wish to stop them dismounting, I would suggest using the dismount event as well.
Called when a player opens their advancements menu, through the ESC menu, or by pressing L.

Checks whether an entity can be damaged by fire. Nether entities and things with fire resistance can't be.
This only counts default Minecraft behaviour.
If you have some strange plugin or system, it might miss it!

Checks whether an entity can be interacted with, in some way. For example, feeding it, breeding it, mounting it, equipping it, etc.
Most entities are.

Checks whether an entity is in water. This includes waterlogged blocks and partially-submerged entities.
Remember that any intersection of the hitbox with water will cause this.

Checks whether an entity is in water (see Is in Water), or under rainfall. Useful to check if players can use their riptide/channelling enchantments!
Also, might be useful for some kind of enderman check.

This allows you to enable/disable a recipe of a merchant, villager or wandering trader.

Disabling a recipe sets the max uses to 0, meaning it will never restock.
Enabling it sets the max uses to 99999. You might exercise caution when using this with ordinary villagers.

Reminder:
Recipe indices go 0, 1, 2, ... like inventory slots. You cannot toggle a trade unless the index exists.
p:
enable (trade|recipe) %number% of [merchant] %merchant%
enable (trade|recipe) %number% of [(villager|[wandering ]trader)] %entity%
disable (trade|recipe) %number% of [merchant] %merchant%
disable (trade|recipe) %number% of [(villager|[wandering ]trader)] %entity%

d:
Replace all of a merchant or villager/wandering trader's current recipes with a new list of merchant recipes.

Note: setting to an empty list will clear the trades.
You may still force-open the window, but you will not be able to open it by manually clicking a villager.
p:
set [(the|all)] (trade|recipe)[s] of [merchant] %merchant% to %merchantrecipes%
set [(the|all)] (trade|recipe)[s] of [(villager|[wandering ]trader)] %entity% to %merchantrecipes%

d:
Forces an entity to illegally mount another.
This can be used to put multiple passengers on a single vehicle, which is not possible using the Bukkit API.
Be careful! All of these entities will send vehicle steer packets to the server. Use it wisely.

Make any entity properly invisible. Does not use packets or potions.
Similar to an armour stand's {Invisible:1b} NBT tag.

Sometimes, this doesn't work in the same tick as spawning the entity.

Move an entity to a location/entity. This will take all passengers/hangers-on with the entity.
It is effectively the same as setting the actual coordinates of the entity within its NBT, but much safer to use.
The effect will not work if they are not in the same world.
This also prevents the screen flickering/jittering that is caused by teleporting a player.
It does not affect velocity or path goals.

Example Uses:
Teleporting entities with multiple, stacked passengers.
Making armour stands follow an entity.
Altering the yaw/pitch of a player without changing velocity.
Alter an entity's swimming state, to true or false.
Make an entity silent (true) or noisy (false).

Called when a horse jumps. Possibly only if the jump is triggered by the rider (rather than a wild horse jumping up and down).
The event-entity is the horse.
Use passengers of event-entity to get the rider(s).

Clears the trade list of a merchant/villager/wandering trader.
As of MiSK 1.0.0, you no longer need to specify whether the object is a merchant or a villager.

The same effect can be achieved by setting the trades to an empty list.

Set the villager's experience. This works through the progress bar that is displayed in their inventory.

When the bar is filled, the villager will level up.

Sets the level of a villager.
The level goes from 1 to 5 and represents the villager's rank and the badge displayed on their belt. When a villager levels up they will gain new trades.

Hint: When creating a villager with custom recipes, set the level to 5 to prevent the trades being lost/type changing due to Minecraft's profession system.

Sets the profession of a villager. The profession takes a capitalised string.

Valid professions list can be found here:
https://papermc.io/javadocs/paper/1.14/org/bukkit/entity/Villager.Profession.html

Reminders:
Level 1 villagers with no exp will automatically lose/re-assign their profession, due to Minecraft's behaviour.
A villager's trades will only restock if they have access to a suitable profession "block." See Minecraft's wiki for more info.

Sets the type (biome-oriented skin) of a villager.
This accepts a capitalised string.

Valid types:
DESERT, JUNGLE, PLAINS, SAVANNA, SNOW, SWAMP, TAIGA

Cancels the current navigation/pathfinding action.
This may not affect targeting goals.

Add a (list of) merchant recipes to a merchant or villager/wandering trader.

Note: before 1.0.0, the object type must be explicit

Set an individual trade of a merchant or villager/wandering trader to a singular merchant recipe.
Reminder:
Recipe indices go 0, 1, 2, ... like inventory slots. You cannot set a trade unless the index already exists.
If you need to add a new trade, please use the ADD syntax.
p:
set trade %number% of [merchant] %merchant% to %merchantrecipe%
set trade %number% of [(villager|[wandering ]trader)] %entity% to %merchantrecipe%

d: returns a map state that can be sent to a player
p:
map state (of|from) %mapcanvases%
map state of %images% [at [(][x[ ]]%number%,[ ][y[ ]]%number%[)]] on %map%

d: Checks if a the provided signs are waxed.
Reverses given list.

t: on Inventory Slot Drop - Drop Info
d: Checks if the item is being dropped from the cursor or the inventory, if the whole stack is being dropped or both.
p:
[the] item is[( not|n't)] [being|getting] dropped from the (cursor|inventory)
[the] (whole|full) stack is[( not|n't)] [being|getting] dropped [from the (cursor|inventory)]

d:
Closes an anvil gui.
*NOTES*:
- Won't do anything if the anvil gui is not open.
- Must be used before opening another anvil gui for a player.

Index of an an inventory slot. Other types of slots may or may not have indices. Note that comparing slots with numbers is also possible; if index of slot is same as the number, comparisonsucceeds. This expression is mainly for the cases where you must for some reason save the slot numbers.

Raw index of slot is unique for the view, see Minecraft Wiki

Represents the biome in ChunkData.
The first pattern is used to set the biome in the `biome gen` section.
The second pattern is used to retrieve biomes in the `chunk gen` and `block pop` sections.
NOTE: The vector represents the position of the biome in the chunk, not the world.
Checks whether the given players have a server resource pack loaded. Please note that this can't detect player's own resource pack, only the resource pack that sent by the server.
p:
%players% (has|have) [a] resource pack [(loaded|installed)]
%players% (doesn't|does not|do not|don't) have [a] resource pack [(loaded|installed)]

d: The spawn point of a world.
Special number values, namely NaN, Infinity and -Infinity
Whether the navigator of a Citizens NPC is paused.

Request that the player's client download and switch resource packs. The client will download 
the resource pack in the background, and will automatically switch to it once the download is complete. 
The URL must be a direct download link.

The hash is used for caching, the player won't have to re-download the resource pack that way. 
The hash must be SHA-1, you can get SHA-1 hash of your resource pack using 
this online tool.

The resource pack request action can be used to check 
status of the sent resource pack request.
p:
send [the] resource pack [from [[the] URL]] %string% to %players%
send [the] resource pack [from [[the] URL]] %string% with hash %string% to %players%

d: Make a Citizens NPC targetable by other mobs.
p:
(citizen|npc)[s] %npcs% (is|can be|are) target(able|ed)
(citizen|npc)[s] %npcs% (is(n't| not)|are(n't| not)|can('t|not be)) target(able|ed)

d: The affected entities in the area cloud effect event.

Opens an anvil GUI to one or more players.
*NOTES*:
- Anvil GUIs are always opened 1 tick later.
- You can only open an anvil GUI for a player if the player is not already viewing another (or this) anvil GUI.
- Using `force` will forcibly close the anvil GUI the player is currently viewing to open a new one. Be aware that, due to how anvil GUIs work, closing an anvil GUI is global, meaning it will be closed for all players viewing it. To avoid this, ensure that you create per-player anvil GUIs.
Directly damages an item. In MC versions 1.12.2 and lower, this can be used to apply data values to items/blocks
Returns the last resource pack response received from a player.
p:
[the] [last] resource pack response[s] of %players%
%players%'[s] [last] resource pack response[s]

d: The message above and below the player list in the tab menu.
The teleport cause within a player teleport event.

p:
[(the|all [[of] the])] (indexes|indices) of %objects%
%objects%'[s] (indexes|indices)
[sorted] (indices|indexes) of %objects% in (ascending|descending) order
[sorted] %objects%'[s] (indices|indexes) in (ascending|descending) order

d: The state in a resource pack request response event.
The category of a sound, they are used for sound options of Minecraft. See the play sound and stop sound effects.
Hides a player from the hover list and decreases the online players count (only if the player count wasn't changed before).
p:
hide %players% (in|on|from) [the] server list
hide %players%'[s] info[rmation] (in|on|from) [the] server list

d: Launch firework effects at the given location(s).

t: Cooldown Time/Remaining Time/Elapsed Time/Last Usage/Bypass Permission
d:
Only usable in command events. Represents the cooldown time, the remaining time, the elapsed time,
the last usage date, or the cooldown bypass permission.
p:
[the] remaining [time] [of [the] (cooldown|wait) [(of|for) [the] [current] command]]
[the] elapsed [time] [of [the] (cooldown|wait) [(of|for) [the] [current] command]]
[the] ((cooldown|wait) time|[wait] time of [the] (cooldown|wait) [(of|for) [the] [current] command])
[the] last usage [date] [of [the] (cooldown|wait) [(of|for) [the] [current] command]]
[the] [cooldown] bypass perm[ission] [of [the] (cooldown|wait) [(of|for) [the] [current] command]]

d: Temperature at given block.

Breaks the block and spawns items as if a player had mined it

You can add a tool, which will spawn items based on how that tool would break the block 
(ie: When using a hand to break stone, it drops nothing, whereas with a pickaxe it drops cobblestone)
Called when a player tries to break a block.
Returns the all claim IDs
Returns the claim IDs of claims at a location.
Returns the claim IDs of the claims of a player.
Returns the remaining claim blocks of a player.

Prevents an anvil gui from closing.
*NOTES*:
- This effect must be used *BEFORE* the gui is opened; if you need to use it after it's already opened you'll have to open it again.
- If this effect is used you'll need to use the `Anvil GUI - Close` effect to close the gui.
Checks whether an item is a block.
Changes whether one or more blocks or blockstates are waterlogged.
Represents a Panda's main or hidden gene. See genetics for more info.
Checks whether or not the provided nbt compounds are empty.
Converts given date to Unix timestamp. This is roughly how many seconds have elapsed since 1 January 1970.
Arithmetic expressions for vectors.
Checks state of the resource pack in a resource pack request response event.
The UUID of a player, entity or world.
returns the scale, center x or z location, or id of a map

t: Players are Visible in Server Ping
d: Whether the player count and possibly a player preview will be displayed by the specified server ping. If this is false, instead of a player count and possibly a player preview, the specified server ping will display "???" where the player count should be.

Puts all of the information stored inside the specified jsonobject into the specified list variable. This is needed as storing json data in list variables is currently the only way to manipulate information in ThatPacketAddon's jsonobjects other than raw string manipulation.
p:
put json %jsonobject% in listvar %objects%
put jsons %jsonobjects% in listvar %objects%

d: Makes the server simulate receiving the specified packet(s) from the specified player(s)
Sends the specified packet(s) to the specified player(s).
An expression for the game profile corresponding to the specified profile.

An expression for a game profile with the specified name and optionally the specified UUID and skin. If the UUID is not specified a random UUID will be created and used. If the skin is not specified the profile will not have a skin associated with it.
An expression for the name of the specified profile.
An expression for the skin of the specified profile.
An expression for the UUID of the specified profile.
An expression for a jsonobject constructed from the information stored inside the specified list variable.

An expression for an enum field of a packet (first see the Packet Info o expression for a more general explanation of packet fields). The specified string is the name of the enum you are getting/setting. Using arbitrary gives you access to all enum fields, rather than just one particular type, and allows you to access certain enums that are NMS types rather than ProtocolLib and thus can't be accessed by their name.

An expression for the packet field of either the specified type or referred to by the specified string, with the specified index, of the specified packet. For example, 'string' can be used as the specified type to get a string field in the specified packet. 'array' must be included in the syntax when the fields are plural (ex. 'string array'). Use 'object' in order to access all fields of the packet. However, some fields will have objects in raw forms that cannot be easily used in Skript without the use of addons such as skript-mirror. Many of these fields can be converted into Skript types; most of these fields must be referred to by a certain string rather than a type: "uuid", "material", "blockdata", "collection" array, "bytebuffer" array (The last two are plural and must have array included in the syntax.

An expression for certain fields of packets (first see the Packet Info expression for a more general explanation) that don't have equivalent types in Skript, and thus must be represented in the form of a jsonobject. The names of the fields can be written as strings but don't have to be. Current accept JSON infos: 'chatcomponent', 'serverping', 'datawatcher', 'watchablecollection', 'gameprofile', 'nbt', 'chatcomponent' array, 'playerinfodata' array.

t: Legacy Number Array Field of Packet
d: An expression for int array and byte array fields of packets. First see the Packet Info expression for a more general explanation of packet fields.
An expression for different kinds of number fields of packets. First see the Packet Info expression for a more general explanation of packet fields.
An expression for the key string of the specified minecraft key.
p:
key of minecraft key %minecraftkey%
minecraft key %minecraftkey%'s key

d: For more information on skript-reflect, you can visit the wiki here: https://tpgamesnl.gitbook.io/skript-reflect/
Gets or sets the length of a vector.
An expression for the prefix of the specified minecraft key.
p:
prefix of minecraft key %minecraftkey%
minecraft key %minecraftkey%'s prefix

d: An expression for a new packet of the specified type.
An expression for the packettype of the specified packet.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's byte array fields. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's byte buffer fields. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's block fields. Note that this is represented in Skript as an itemstack with the material of the block. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's blockdata fields. Note that this is represented in Skript as an itemstack with the material and data of the blockdata. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's boolean fields. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.
The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's byte fields. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

t: Chat Component Array Field of Packet
d: The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's chat component array fields. The chat components are represented as a jsonobject. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's chat component fields. The chat component is represented as a jsonobject. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's chattype fields. This is an automatically generated enum packet field, and is represented in Skript as a string (the name of the value of the field). The returned name will always be in uppercase, however when setting this expression the cases used do not matter.The different values of this enum are "CHAT", "SYSTEM", "GAME_INFO". The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's chatvisibility fields. This is an automatically generated enum packet field, and is represented in Skript as a string (the name of the value of the field). The returned name will always be in uppercase, however when setting this expression the cases used do not matter.The different values of this enum are "FULL", "SYSTEM", "HIDDEN". The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's clientcommand fields. This is an automatically generated enum packet field, and is represented in Skript as a string (the name of the value of the field). The returned name will always be in uppercase, however when setting this expression the cases used do not matter.The different values of this enum are "PERFORM_RESPAWN", "REQUEST_STATS", "OPEN_INVENTORY_ACHIEVEMENT". The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's collection fields. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's string fields. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.
Creates a vector between two locations.
Gets the squared length of a vector.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's hand fields. This is an automatically generated enum packet field, and is represented in Skript as a string (the name of the value of the field). The returned name will always be in uppercase, however when setting this expression the cases used do not matter. The different values of this enum are "MAIN_HAND", "OFF_HAND". The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.
Check if a bound intersects with another bound or a potential bound at 2 locations.
p:
%bound% (intersects with|overlaps) %location% and %location%
%bound% (intersects with|overlaps) %bound%
%bound% doesn't (intersect with|overlap) %location% and %location%
%bound% doesn't (intersect with|overlap) %bound%

d: The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's difficulty fields. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's direction fields. This is an automatically generated enum packet field, and is represented in Skript as a string (the name of the value of the field). The returned name will always be in uppercase, however when setting this expression the cases used do not matter.The different values of this enum are "DOWN", "UP", "NORTH", "SOUTH", "WEST", "EAST". The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's double fields. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's dimension id fields. 0 represents the overworld, -1 represents the nether, and 1 represents the end. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's entityuseaction fields. This is an automatically generated enum packet field, and is represented in Skript as a string (the name of the value of the field). The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's NBT list fields. The NBT values contained int list, as well as any NBT values contained in those NBT values, are represented as a jsonobject with three parts:
The name, with index/key "name", which is just the name (sometimes null)
The type, with index/key "type", which is the type of NBT, and can have the following values:
- "byte", "short", "int", "long", "float", "double", the basic number types
- "string", for strings
- "byte_array" and "int_array", for basic number array types
- "compound", for an NBT compound
- "list_something", such as "list_string" or "list_compound", for a list of some other type
The value, with index/key "value", which is the actual value:
- In the cases of the number types and string type this is just the number/string (when accessing using a list variable in Skript it's like {_nbt::value}
- In the case of the number array types and the list type this is an array of the objects (when accessing using a list variable in Skript it's like {_nbt::value::*}, ex. {_nbt::value::1} for the first element)
- In the case of the compound it's a JSONObject of the NBT values, each also having its own name, type and value (when accessing through a list variable in Skript it's: {_nbt::value::example_name::name} for the name (which is the same as the index, so it will be "example_name"), {_nbt::value::example_name::type} for the type, let's say "string", and {_nbt::value::example_name::value} for the actual value, let's say "pie") The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.
The value of the field numbered at the specified index of the specified packet's fields. The index defaults to 0 if unspecified and the packet expression defaults to event-packet if unspecified.

The latency contained in this playerinfodata, respresented as an integer amount of milliseconds. Different values of latency will result in different latency bar icons being displayed if the specified playerinfodata is used as a tab in the tablist:
latency < 0 ms will show a red X

Not an actual event, but rather a group of packet field aliases to be used with packets of the specified packettype. Packet info aliases are aliases for specific usages of packet info expressions. Under the main scope lines are written in the form '<new syntax> = <old syntax>', where the old syntax is how you would normally write an expression for the desired packet info, and the new syntax is how you want to be able to write it. Note that the new syntax is essentially being registered as a Skript syntax, so you can write it with features of Skript syntax like optional parts enclosed in '[]', and multiple usages of '%packet%' are allowed in your syntax, though only one of them should be possible to use at a time since only one packet is used to evaluate the alias Your syntax does not have to require or even allow the usage of '%packet%'. In this case, 'event-packet' will be used as the packet. A small addition to normal Skript syntax is that now, if you would like to have a group of different options for syntax but also have the whole thing be optional, instead of writing '[(a|b|...)]' you can write '[a|b|...]'.

Called when a packet of one of the specified types is being sent or received. You can optionally specify a priority; triggers with higher priority will be called later (so high priority will come after low priority, and monitor priority will come last). By default, the priority is normal.

d:
Parses text as a given type, or as a given pattern.
This expression can be used in two different ways: One which parses the entire text as a single instance of a type, e.g. as a number, and one that parses the text according to a pattern.
If the given text could not be parsed, this expression will return nothing and the parse error will be set if some information is available.
Some notes about parsing with a pattern:
- The pattern must be a Skript pattern, e.g. percent signs are used to define where to parse which types, e.g. put a %number% or %items% in the pattern if you expect a number or some items there.
- You have to save the expression's value in a list variable, e.g. set {parsed::*} to message parsed as "...".
- The list variable will contain the parsed values from all %types% in the pattern in order. If a type was plural, e.g. %items%, the variable's value at the respective index will be a list variable, e.g. the values will be stored in {parsed::1::*}, not {parsed::1}.

An entity is something in a world that's not a block, e.g. a player, a skeleton, or a zombie, but also projectiles like arrows, fireballs or thrown potions, or special entities like dropped items, falling blocks or paintings.

Forms a 'cylindrical shaped' vector using yaw to manipulate the current point.
Forms a 'spherical shaped' vector using yaw and pitch to manipulate the current point.

A logarithm, with base 10 if none is specified. This is the inverse operation to exponentiation (for positive bases only), i.e. log(base ^ exponent, base) = exponent for any positive number 'base' and any number 'exponent'.
Another useful equation is base ^ log(a, base) = a for any numbers 'base' and 'a'.
Please note that due to how numbers are represented in computers, these equations do not hold for all numbers, as the computed values may slightly differ from the correct value.
Returns NaN (not a number) if any of the arguments are negative.

t: Custom request from other Bungee server

Set the response of a custom request
Called when a chunk is unloaded due to not being near any player.
Checks whether an entity has AI.
Checks whether a player is blocking with their shield.

Called when an entity is damaged by a block.
Anything that works in vanilla Skript's damage event (victim/damage cause/damage/final damage)
will all work in this event too.

`victim` = Same as vanilla Skript, `victim` is used to get the damaged entity.

`event-block` = The block that damaged the entity
Checks to see if an entity is currently leashed.
Checks to see if an entity is currently using the Riptide enchantment.
Checks whether an entity is silent i.e. its sounds are disabled.
Check if an entity is tameable.

The amount of time a player has played for on the server. This info is stored in the player's statistics in the main world's data folder. Changing this will also change the player's stats which can be views in the client's statistics menu.
Using this expression on offline players on Minecraft 1.14 and below will return nothing <none>.
Charges or uncharges a creeper. A creeper is charged when it has been struck by lightning.
Starts the explosion process of a creeper or instantly explodes it.
p:
instantly explode [creeper[s]] %livingentities%
explode [creeper[s]] %livingentities% instantly
ignite creeper[s] %livingentities%
start (ignition|explosion) [process] of [creeper[s]] %livingentities%
stop (ignition|explosion) [process] of [creeper[s]] %livingentities%

d: Called when clicking on inventory slot.
Called when an item is damaged. Most tools are damaged by using them; armor is damaged when the wearer takes damage.
Checks if a block is indirectly or directly powered by redstone
p:
%blocks% (is|are) redstone powered
%blocks% (is|are) indirectly redstone powered
%blocks% (is|are)(n't| not) redstone powered
%blocks% (is|are)(n't| not) indirectly redstone powered

d: Called when a sponge absorbs blocks.
Check if an item has a custom model data tag
p:
%itemtypes% (has|have) [custom] model data [floats|flags|strings|colo[u]rs]
%itemtypes% (doesn't|does not|do not|don't) have [custom] model data [floats|flags|strings|colo[u]rs]

d: Gets the status of a server on the proxy

Creates an explosion of a given force. The Minecraft Wiki has an article on explosions which lists the explosion forces of TNT, creepers, etc.
Hint: use a force of 0 to create a fake explosion that does no damage whatsoever, or use the explosion effect introduced in Skript 2.0.
Starting with Bukkit 1.4.5 and Skript 2.0 you can use safe explosions which will damage entities but won't destroy any blocks.
p:
[(create|make)] [an] explosion (of|with) (force|strength|power) %number% [%directions% %locations%] [(with fire)]
[(create|make)] [a] safe explosion (of|with) (force|strength|power) %number% [%directions% %locations%]
[(create|make)] [a] fake explosion [%directions% %locations%]
[(create|make)] [an] explosion[ ]effect [%directions% %locations%]

d: Controls whether or not an entity is silent.
p:
silence %entities%
unsilence %entities%
make %entities% silent
make %entities% not silent

d: The blocks absorbed by a sponge block.
Copy of given text in Lowercase, Uppercase, Proper Case, camelCase, PascalCase, Snake_Case, and Kebab-Case
p:
%strings% in (upper|lower)[ ]case
(upper|lower)[ ]case %strings%
capitali(s|z)ed %strings%
%strings% in [(lenient|strict) ](proper|title)[ ]case
[(lenient|strict) ](proper|title)[ ]case %strings%
%strings% in [(lenient|strict) ]camel[ ]case
[(lenient|strict) ]camel[ ]case %strings%
%strings% in [(lenient|strict) ]pascal[ ]case
[(lenient|strict) ]pascal[ ]case %strings%
%strings% in [(lower|upper|capital|screaming)[ ]]snake[ ]case
[(lower|upper|capital|screaming)[ ]]snake[ ]case %strings%
%strings% in [(lower|upper|capital)[ ]]kebab[ ]case
[(lower|upper|capital)[ ]]kebab[ ]case %strings%

d: Returns whether an entity has AI.
The owner of a tameable entity, such as a horse or wolf.
The hotbar button clicked in an inventory click event.
Returns a line break separator.

The maximum durability of an item. Changing requires Minecraft 1.20.5+
Note: 'delete' will remove the max durability from the item (making it a non-damageable item). Delete requires Paper 1.21+
The number of uppercase, lowercase, or digit characters in a string.
p:
number of upper[ ]case char(acters|s) in %string%
number of lower[ ]case char(acters|s) in %string%
number of digit char(acters|s) in %string%

d: Power of a redstone block
The number of ticks that an entity is invulnerable to damage for.
Get all the blocks that were destroyed in an explode event. Supports add/remove/set/clear/delete blocks.
Makes an entity invulnerable/not invulnerable.

t: Anvil GUI - All Open Anvil Gui
d: Gets all the open anvil guis.
Get an item with custom model data.
The type of a fireworkeffect.
Destroy a citizen by id.

Returns true if the http client has been closed/shutdown.
Required Java 21+

Closes/shutdowns an http client
Required Java 21+
Stops/starts a http server.
p:
start [http[ server]] %httpservers%
(stop|shut[ ]down) [http[ server]] %httpservers% [with delay %integer%]
close http[ ]exchange %httpexchanges%

d: The distance an entity has fallen for.
The enchantment bonus in an enchant prepare event. This represents the number of bookshelves affecting/surrounding the enchantment table.
Checks if an entity will create fire when it explodes. This condition is also usable in an explosion prime event.
p:
%entities% ((is|are) incendiary|cause[s] a[n] (incendiary|fiery) explosion)
%entities% ((is not|are not|isn't|aren't) incendiary|(does not|do not|doesn't|don't) cause[s] a[n] (incendiary|fiery) explosion)
the [event(-| )]explosion (is|(is not|isn't)) (incendiary|fiery)

d: Sets if an entity's explosion will leave behind fire. This effect is also usable in an explosion prime event.

The applied enchantments in an enchant event.
 Deleting or removing the applied enchantments will prevent the item's enchantment.

The enchant item in an enchant prepare event or enchant event.
It can be modified, but enchantments will still be applied in the enchant event.
The enchantment offer in enchant prepare events.
p:
[all [of]] [the] enchant[ment] offers
enchant[ment] offer[s] %numbers%
[the] %number%(st|nd|rd|th) enchant[ment] offer

d:
The percentage of exploded blocks dropped in an explosion event.
When changing the yield, a value greater than 1 will function the same as using 1.
Attempting to change the yield to a value less than 0 will have no effect.

The yield of the explosion in an explosion prime event. This is how big the explosion is.
 When changing the yield, values less than 0 will be ignored.
 Read this wiki page for more information
The gamerule value of a world.

The player's progress in reaching the next level, this represents the experience bar in the game. Please note that this value is between 0 and 1 (e.g. 0.5 = half experience bar).
Changing this value can cause the player's level to change if the resulting level progess is negative or larger than 1, e.g. increase the player's level progress by 0.5 will make the player gain a level if their progress was more than 50%.
Gets a parameter by it's key.

The numerical value of an entity's particular attribute.
Note that the movement speed attribute cannot be reliably used for players. For that purpose, use the speed expression instead.
Resetting an entity's attribute is only available in Minecraft 1.11 and above.

The cost of an enchantment offer. This is displayed to the right of an enchantment offer.
If the cost is changed, it will always be at least 1.
This changes how many levels are required to enchant, but does not change the number of levels removed.
To change the number of levels removed, use the enchant event.

How much damage is done in a entity/vehicle/item damage events.
For entity damage events, possibly ignoring armour, criticals and/or enchantments (remember that in Skript '1' is one full heart, not half a heart).
For items, it's the amount of durability damage the item will be taking.
Despawn or respawn a citizen.
Effectively an alias of 'y-coordinate of …', it represents the height of some location within the world.
Builds a HTTP client.
The enchantmentoffer in an enchant prepare event.
A wrapper for the value of a gamerule for a world.

Called when a player successfully enchants an item.
 To get the enchanted item, see the enchant item expression
Stops or restarts the server. If restart is used when the restart-script spigot.yml option isn't defined, the server will stop instead.
The blocks fertilized in block fertilize events.
When a player last/first logged in the server. 'last login' requires paper to get the last login, otherwise it will get the last time they were seen on the server.

Creates a new websocket.
# Note:
Use WebsocketOpenEvent in order to do actions when opened.
p: (register|make|send) [a] [new] websocket request with [ur(l|i)] %string% and store it in %object%

d: Block data is the detailed information about a block, referred to in Minecraft as BlockStates, allowing for the manipulation of different aspects of the block, including shape, waterlogging, direction the block is facing, and so much more. Information regarding each block's optional data can be found on Minecraft's Wiki. Find the block you're looking for and scroll down to 'Block States'. Different states must be separated by a semicolon (see examples). The 'minecraft:' namespace is optional, as well as are underscores.
The heal reason of a heal event.

*NOTES*:

Checks whether a block is passable.
A block is passable if it has no colliding parts that would prevent players from moving through it.
Blocks like tall grass, flowers, signs, etc. are passable, but open doors, fence gates, trap doors, etc. are not because they still have parts that can be collided with.

d:
Makes a living entity attack an entity with a melee attack.
Using 'attack' will make the attacker use the item in their main hand and will apply extra data from the item, including enchantments and attributes.
Using 'damage' with a number of hearts will not account for the item in the main hand and will always be the number provided.
p:
make %livingentities% attack %entities%
force %livingentities% to attack %entities%
make %livingentities% damage %entities% by %number% [heart[s]]
force %livingentities% to damage %entities% by %number% [heart[s]]

All numbers between two given numbers, useful for looping.
Use 'numbers' if your start is not an integer and you want to keep the fractional part of the start number constant, or use 'integers' if you only want to loop integers.
You may also use 'decimals' if you want to use the decimal precision of the start number.
You may want to use the 'times' expression instead, for instance 'loop 5 times:'

 Modifying the repair amount will affect how much experience is given to the player after mending.

A projectile's critical state. The only currently accepted projectiles are arrows and tridents.

Allows a connection from the `on connect` event, this means that the player attempting to join will be able to join the server. This will mean that they can bypass the whitelist and any other connections that end with them being disconnected.

Returns whether or not the consumable item should be consumed in the Entity Shoot Event.

A very general condition, it simply compares two values. Usually you can only compare for equality (e.g. block is/isn't of <type>), but some values can also be compared using greater than/less than. In that case you can also test for whether an object is between two others.
Note: This is the only element where not all patterns are shown. It has actually another two sets of similar patters, but with (was|were) or will be instead of (is|are) respectively, which check different time states of the first expression.
p:
(neither|) %objects% ((is|are)(|(n't| not| neither)) ((greater|more|higher|bigger|larger) than|above)|\>) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% ((is|are)(|(n't| not| neither)) (greater|more|higher|bigger|larger|above) [than] or (equal to|the same as)|\>=) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% ((is|are)(|(n't| not| neither)) ((less|smaller|lower) than|below)|\<) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% ((is|are)(|(n't| not| neither)) (less|smaller|lower|below) [than] or (equal to|the same as)|\<=) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% ((is|are) (not|neither)|isn't|aren't|!=) [equal to] %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (is|are|=) [(equal to|the same as)] %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (is|are) between %objects% and %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (is not|are not|isn't|aren't) between %objects% and %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (was|were)(|(n't| not| neither)) ((greater|more|higher|bigger|larger) than|above) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (was|were)(|(n't| not| neither)) (greater|more|higher|bigger|larger|above) [than] or (equal to|the same as) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (was|were)(|(n't| not| neither)) ((less|smaller|lower) than|below) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (was|were)(|(n't| not| neither)) (less|smaller|lower|below) [than] or (equal to|the same as) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% ((was|were) (not|neither)|wasn't|weren't) [equal to] %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (was|were) [(equal to|the same as)] %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (was|were) between %objects% and %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (was not|were not|wasn't|weren't) between %objects% and %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (will be|(will (not|neither) be|won't be)) ((greater|more|higher|bigger|larger) than|above) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (will be|(will (not|neither) be|won't be)) (greater|more|higher|bigger|larger|above) [than] or (equal to|the same as) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (will be|(will (not|neither) be|won't be)) ((less|smaller|lower) than|below) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (will be|(will (not|neither) be|won't be)) (less|smaller|lower|below) [than] or (equal to|the same as) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% ((will (not|neither) be|won't be)|(isn't|aren't|is not|are not) (turning|changing) [in]to) [equal to] %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (will be [(equal to|the same as)]|(is|are) (turning|changing) [in]to) %objects%
(neither|) %objects% will be between %objects% and %objects%
(neither|) %objects% (will not be|won't be) between %objects% and %objects%

d:
Check if a plugin is enabled/disabled on the server.
Plugin names can be found in the plugin's 'plugin.yml' file or by using the '/plugins' command, they are NOT the name of the plugin's jar file.
When checking if a plugin is not enabled, this will return true if the plugin is either disabled or not on the server. 
When checking if a plugin is disabled, this will return true if the plugin is on the server and is disabled.
p:
plugin[s] %strings% (is|are) enabled
plugin[s] %strings% (is|are)(n't| not) enabled
plugin[s] %strings% (is|are) disabled

An entity type with an amount, e.g. '2 zombies'. I might remove this type in the future and make a more general 'type' type, i.e. a type that has a number and a type.

The protocol version that will be sent as the protocol version of the server in a server list ping event. For more information and list of protocol versions visit wiki.vg.
If this protocol version doesn't match with the protocol version of the client, the client will see the version string.
But please note that, this expression has no visual effect over the version string. For example if the server uses PaperSpigot 1.12.2, and you make the protocol version 107 (1.9),
the version string will not be "Paper 1.9", it will still be "Paper 1.12.2".
But then you can customize the version string as you wish.
Also if the protocol version of the player is higher than protocol version of the server, it will say
"Server out of date!", and if vice-versa "Client out of date!" when you hover on the ping bars.

This can be set in a server list ping event only
(increase and decrease effects cannot be used because that wouldn't make sense).

The count of max players. This can be changed in a server list ping event only.
'real max players' returns the real count of max players of the server and can be modified on Paper 1.16 or later.

In 1.20.5+, the maximum stack size of items can be changed to any integer from 1 to 99, and stacked up to the maximum stack size of the inventory they're in.

A player. Depending on whether a player is online or offline several actions can be performed with them, though you won't get any errors when using effects that only work if the player is online (e.g. changing their inventory) on an offline player.
You have two possibilities to use players as command arguments: <player> and <offline player>. The first requires that the player is online and also accepts only part of the name, while the latter doesn't require that the player is online, but the player's name has to be entered exactly.

A timespan is a difference of two different dates or times, e.g '10 minutes'. Timespans are always displayed as real life time, but can be defined as minecraft time, e.g. '5 minecraft days and 12 hours'.

Convert (and check) JSON string to a flat file data.
This will create the desired path, and if exist, will override it.
If the inputted JSON is not valid, no exception will be thrown instead of a whole stack trace.
The specified variable will be clear if no error was found, else it will contains the error message.

Shortcut are completely optional and here to help you gain time while coding.
Instead of specifying the whole file path every time, simple load a shortcut key, which will be replaced by the desired file.
If you create a shortcut for an already existing one, it will be overed.
The private mark will make the shortcut only available in the current script only, to avoid conflict with other potential scripts.

Start a new queue for a specific file path.
Every change made with that path will be queued, and not saved directly.
You will either need to save the queue (make every save) or clear it (cancel every changes) to return to a normal state.
This system is recommended when you are managing a huge quantity of data in a single time.
Get or set a datawatcher's index. Used for metadata packet. More informations here https://github.com/Anarchick/skript-packet/wiki/Data-Watcher

Stop or clear the queue for a specific path.

You can also clear the queue, and therefore every changes will be lost.

Represent the header at the top of an YAML file.
This property can only work with YAML files, and will simply do nothing if used with JSON or TOML
The comment # are already included, means you should not add them yourself!
The framed mark will simply make the header text between a frame, looking more professional.

Represent a node value.
You can get the value from its key, shortcut file are allowed here.
You can set, remove and remove all keys on that property.
In order to keep the comments, for Yaml files, only header & first-layer nodes comments are allowed. Every other will be deleted!

The default mark is only used in set changer (when you set a value).
If the specified node is already set to something, then it'll not override it and do nothing.
Useful when generating default tree for configuration files, and / or updating it while keeping old values.

Return the string list of every nodes the files have, with a specific layer of not.
Imagine a second you have this file (YAML file for example, but works with other types too):

First:
    Second: Hello World!
    Third:
        Fourth: 50
    Lastly: 30
SecondMain: Welcome!

Then, the following code will returns:
node section "First" of "config" # [Second, Third.Fourth, Lastly]
node section "First" of "config" as one layer # [Second, Third, Lastly]
node section "" of "config" # [First.Lastly, First.Third.Fourth, First.Second, SecondMain]
node section "" of "config" as one layer # [First, SecondMain]

It only works with getter, so you can't set that to anything. Use the 'value XX of XX' property to change them.
This expression will try to get the desired node, and will if the value is not set:
- If the 'set' mark is specified, set the default value provided and return it
- Else, only return the default value provided without changing the file

Represent the comment linked to a node in an YAML file.

Find the closest nearby structure of a given structure type. Will not be set if not found. Finding unexplored structures can, and will, block if the world is looking in chunks that gave not generated yet. This can lead to the world temporarily freezing while locating an unexplored structure.
The radius is not a rigid square radius. Each structure may alter how many chunks to check for each iteration. Do not assume that only a radius x radius chunk area will be checked. For example, a woodland mansion can potentially check up to 20,000 blocks away (or more) regardless of the radius used.
This will not load or generate chunks. This can also lead to instances where the server can hang if you are only looking for unexplored structures. This is because it will keep looking further and further out in order to find the structure.

Checks whether an entity has a potion effect with certain properties.
An entity is considered having a potion effect if it has a potion effect with at least the specified properties.
For example, if an entity has an 'ambient speed 5' effect, they would be considered as having 'speed 5'.
For exact comparisons, consider using the Potion Effect of Entity/Item expression in an 'is' comparison.

Checks if the documents contains the specified key.
Checks if the documents contains the specified value.
Checks if the document is empty (if it contains no keys or values).
Quickly create a collection with a specific name in one of your databases. Make sure it does not exist first.

Firstly, you can insert your Mongo document in the collection you want, with an automatically generated unique identifier if not specified or already existing. This will not replace existing documents with the same identifier, but will insert a new one. Secondly, if you want to replace the document, you can use the replace syntax. Finally, to remove the document, simply use remove. This effect is only a shortcut and simply executes a query with "_id" as the unique identifier. If you have changed the unique ID field, you should not use this effect.
Quickly retrieve all the collections contained in a Mongo database.
Quickly retrieve all the databases contained in a Mongo server.

It is sometimes redundant to extract a document from a document from a document etc... and undertake several data manipulations on several lines. To remedy this, this expression will only ask you the path (MongoSK specific syntax, be careful) as follows: "foo.bar", or "foo[0].bar" or "foo.bar[2]" for example. Embedded documents must be separated by a dot, and you must specify in brackets the index of the array (starting from 0) if it is a list.
Retrieve a specific collection via its name from a database on your Mongo server. You must specify the database where your collection is located.
If you want to know the number of documents in a collection, you should use this expression. Please note that this is an estimate.
p:
[the] mongo[(db|sk)] estimated doc[ument][s] count of %mongoskcollections%
%mongoskcollections%'[s] mongo[(db|sk)] estimated doc[ument][s] count

d: This expression is very useful and gives complete freedom to use your database with MongoSK. Indeed, it allows to execute any command to a database, which will return the documents to you. You are therefore no longer dependent on the features of MongoSK, and you can use any commands. To learn more about the possible commands, see: https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/command/.
Retrieve a specific database from a Mongo server. You must specify in which Mongo server your collection is located.

t: Mongo database name of a collection
d: Retrieves the name of the database to which the collection belongs. You can then use the expression to retrieve a database by its name if you wish.
p:
[the] mongo[(sk|db)] database name of %mongoskcollections%
%mongoskcollections%'[s] mongo[(sk|db)] database name

d: The distinct query is useful when you want to retrieve a specific field from each document in a given collection. For example, if you have documents with a "name" field, you can use this expression to retrieve all the "name" fields of the documents in the collection. Useful, isn't it?This expression does not accept constructed queries because there is no need to use it. They can only be used with the find query.

Retrieve the JSON representation of the document. This expression can be useful to use it with skript-json and convert the document to a Skript variable, and vice versa. You can also define the JSON of the document.

This expression is extremely important because it allows you to manage the values and lists of your documents. You can define lists, add items to lists, set values, or delete them. If Skript adapters are enabled, you can specify any type of data, and they will be automatically transformed by MongoSK so that they can be registered. So you can store players, blocks, items for example.
With this expression, retrieve all the keys of a document. The expression returns a list of text.
With this expression, retrieve all the values of a document. The expression returns a list of different data types.
Create custom Mongo filters to target a certain type of data in your queries to your collections.The comparators are the same as for Skript.
p:
[[a] new] mongo[(db|sk)] filter [where] field %string% is ((greater|more|higher|bigger|larger|above) [than] or (equal to|the same as)|\>=) %integer/number%
[[a] new] mongo[(db|sk)] filter [where] field %string% is (((greater|more|higher|bigger|larger) than|above)|\>) %integer/number%
[[a] new] mongo[(db|sk)] filter [where] field %string% is ((less|smaller|below) [than] or (equal to|the same as)|\<=) %integer/number%
[[a] new] mongo[(db|sk)] filter [where] field %string% is (((less|smaller) than|below)|\<) %integer/number%
[[a] new] mongo[(db|sk)] filter [where] field %string% is ((not|neither)|isn't|!=) [equal to] %integer/number/string/boolean/mongoskdocument%
[[a] new] mongo[(db|sk)] filter [where] field %string% (is|equals|=) %integer/number/string/boolean/mongoskdocument%
[[a] new] mongo[(db|sk)] filter [where] field %string% (does(n't| not) exist|is not (set|defined))
[[a] new] mongo[(db|sk)] filter [where] field %string% (exists|is (set|defined))

d: Use this expression if you want to make simple find requests, easily and quickly. To do so, you must specify the type of your query: are you looking for several documents or the first document in the collection? Then, the first means is to use a filter to specify the type of data you are looking for. Finally, you need to specify where the query should ask for the data, i.e. in which collection.The second way is to specify a Mongo request that you will have built beforehand. This will allow you to better specify your request and create a more complex one.You also have the possibility to modify already existing documents and delete documents, in the same way as for making your queries.
p:
[find] (first|all) mongo[(sk|db)] document[s] [(with|by) [filter] %mongoskfilter%] (of|from) collection %mongoskcollection%
[find] (first|all) mongo[(sk|db)] document[s] (of|from) query %mongoskquery%

d: Thanks to this expression, you will be able to combine two filters to refine your queries. Several types of filter comparison are possible, and, or, and nor.
p:
mongo[(sk|db)] joined filters %mongoskfilter% (and|or|nor) %mongoskfilter%
mongo[(sk|db)] joined filters %mongoskfilters% with (and|or|nor) [join] mode

d: Thanks to this expression, you will be able to combine two sorts to refine your queries.
Retrieve the names of different Mongo elements. You can use this expression to retrieve the name of collections or databases.
Defines the collection in which the query will run.
Defines the comment of the request, in order to better monitor the query on your server. This is optional.
Enables writing to temporary files on the server. When set to true, the server can write temporary data to disk while executing the operation. This is optional.
p:
[the] mongo[(db|sk)] disk usage of %mongoskqueries%
%mongoskqueries%'[s] mongo[(db|sk)] disk usage

d: Allows you to set a filter associated with the query. This is optional for find queries, but mandatory for delete or replace queries!
Sets the maximum number of documents to be retrieved through this request. This is optional.
Sets the number of documents to be skipped during the request. This is optional.
Defines the sortings to be applied to the query. This is optional.

Create a connection to your remote MongoDB host using this expression.This syntax requires to specify a valid MongoDB connection string.Don't forget to include the 'appName' option in order to improve MongoSK reports in case of problems and to identify requests to your server monitoring.You can consult the attached examples or the official documentation: https://docs.mongodb.com/manual/reference/connection-string/.
Defines the sortings to be applied to the query. This is optional.
If you want to create a new empty document to specify your data, this expression will do the job.
This expression will allow you to create a new Mongo query, which you can refine with the many expressions available.
Represents a Mongo collection, which contains documents. Use the Mongo Collection expression to create this type.
Represents a Mongo database, which contains collections. If you want to create this type, look at the Mongo Database expression.

Represents a Mongo (BSON) document, which contains some fields and values. If you want to read fields values, use the Mongo Value expression. Otherwise, you can use the Mongo Query expression to query your Mongo collection for documents.
Represents a filter, which can be used in your queries to target a certain type of data. You can create a filter using the Mongo Filter expression.
Allows you to build a complex query to target a very specific type of data with specific options.
Represents a remote MongoDB server, with which you can interact. To create this type, use the Mongo Server expression.
Create a sort to first retrieve certain documents according to the specified fields.

The Mongo section document allows the initialization of a new document and allows to define its fields in a quick and intuitive way with the expression "Mongo section document value". The variable you specify in the first line represents the variable that will contain the document built in the section. You can then use this variable after the end of your section.
p: set %object% to [a] new mongo[(db|sk)] document with

d: This effect can only be used in a Mongo section document, and allows quick assignment of fields to a given document. Be aware that this syntax is only available for basic data manipulation. If your manipulation is not possible via this section, you can perfectly well use the classic field definition expression in addition to this section.

d: Register a new shaped/shapeless recipe for a specific item using custom ingredients. Recipes support items and material choices for ingredients. Material choices allow you to use Minecraft tags or lists of items. The ID will be the name given to this recipe. IDs may only contain letters, numbers, periods, hyphens, a single colon and underscores, NOT SPACES!!! By default, if no namespace is provided, recipes will start with the namespace "minecraft:", this can be changed in the config to whatever you want. IDs are used for recipe discovery/unlocking recipes for players. You may also include an optional group for recipes. These will group the recipes together in the recipe book. NOTE: Recipes with 4 or less ingredients will be craftable in the player's crafting grid.
p: register [new] (shaped|shapeless) recipe for %itemtype% (using|with ingredients) %itemtypes/recipechoices% with id %string% [in group %-string%]

Apply a tool component to any item making it usable tool.
Requires Paper 1.21.3+
See [**Tool Component**](https://minecraft.wiki/w/Data_component_format#tool) on McWiki for more details.

**Entries/Sections**:
- `default_mining_speed` = The default mining speed of this tool, used if no rules override it. Defaults to 1.0. [Optional]
- `damage_per_block` = The amount of durability to remove each time a block is broken with this tool. Must be a non-negative integer. [Optional]
- `can_destroy_blocks_in_creative` = Whether players can break blocks while holding this tool in Creative mode. Defaults to true. [Optional] (Requires MC 1.21.5+)
- `rules:` =  A list of rules for the blocks that this tool has a special behavior with.

Called when a message is broadcasted.

Plays a sound at given location for everyone or just for given players, or plays a sound to specified players. Both Minecraft sound names and Spigot sound names are supported. Playing resource pack sounds are supported too. The sound category is 'master' by default. 

When running 1.19+, playing a sound from an entity directly will result in the sound coming from said entity, even while moving.
If the sound is custom, a location emitter will follow the entity. Do note that pitch and volume 
are reflected based on the entity, and Minecraft may not use the values from this syntax.

Minecraft sometimes has a set of sounds under one sound ID that will randomly play. To counter this, you can directly state which seed to use.

Please note that sound names can get changed in any Minecraft or Spigot version, or even removed from Minecraft itself.
p:
play sound[s] %strings% [[with] seed %number%] [(in|from) %soundcategory%] [(at|with) volume %number%] [(and|at|with) pitch %number%] (at|on|from) %locations/entities% [(to|for) %players%]
play sound[s] %strings% [[with] seed %number%] [(in|from) %soundcategory%] [(at|with) volume %number%] [(and|at|with) pitch %number%] [(to|for) %players%] [(at|on|from) %locations/entities%]

d: Returns the advancement progress of a player.

Sends a title and/or subtitle to an audience with an optional fade in, stay, and/or fade out time.
If sending only the subtitle, it will only be shown if the audience currently has a title displayed. Otherwise, it will be shown when the audience is next shown a title.
Additionally, if no input is given for the times, the previous times of the last sent title will be used (or default values). Use the reset title effect to restore the default values for the times.
p:
send title %object% [with subtitle %object%] [to %audiences%] [for %timespan%] [with fade[(-| )]in %timespan%] [[and] [with] fade[(-| )]out %timespan%]
send subtitle %object% [to %audiences%] [for %timespan%] [with fade[(-| )]in %timespan%] [[and] [with] fade[(-| )]out %timespan%]
